did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically AlexNet, ResNet101, and DenseNet201, combined with DeepLabV3+ for semantic segmentation, to automate the classification of melanoma from dermoscopic images in the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a comparative performance analysis of CNN architectures (AlexNet, ResNet101, DenseNet201) combined with DeepLabV3+ for melanoma detection on the ISIC dataset. It includes specific methodology, data augmentation, training procedures, and quantitative metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparative Performance Analysis in Melanoma Detection with DeeplabV3+ Segmentation
journal: 2025 10th International Conference on Computer and Communication Engineering (ICCCE)
brief_summary: This study evaluates the effectiveness of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for automating melanoma detection using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. The methodology integrates DeepLabV3+ for lesion segmentation with three CNN architectures: AlexNet, ResNet101, and DenseNet201. The pipeline incorporates data augmentation and transfer learning to enhance model generalization and robustness. Performance was assessed using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy of 0.9057, outperforming AlexNet (0.9011) and ResNet101 (0.8644). The results demonstrate that combining DeepLabV3+ segmentation with DenseNet201 offers a viable, supportive tool for early melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection; categorized as malignant. The dataset contained 404 images of melanoma (after oversampling to 1450 for training).
A benign skin lesion class used as a comparator to melanoma. The dataset contained 1450 images of nevus.
A benign skin lesion class used as a comparator to melanoma. The dataset contained 1450 images of seborrheic keratosis (after oversampling from minority class status).

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The study automates the classification of melanoma using dermoscopic images. The CNN models (AlexNet, ResNet101, DenseNet201) classify skin lesions into three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma (malignant), nevus, and seborrheic keratosis. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

DeepLabV3+ is used for semantic segmentation to extract lesion regions and eliminate irrelevant background. The output is a binary mask that isolates the lesion area from the surrounding skin to guide the classification models.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset sourced from Kaggle, created and maintained by Cabmarcos, containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions along with segmentation masks.

use.training: Used for training the CNN models (AlexNet, ResNet101, DenseNet201) after data augmentation and oversampling of minority classes.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation of the models.
use.other: The dataset included segmentation masks which were used to train the DeepLabV3+ model for lesion segmentation prior to classification.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models (DenseNet201, AlexNet, ResNet101) were based on descriptive statistics of classification metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC-ROC) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Melanoma Detection (Kaggle).evaluation_setting: Classification of melanoma vs. non-melanoma (nevus, seborrheic keratosis) using DeepLabV3+ segmentation followed by CNN classification
ISIC Melanoma Detection (Kaggle).notes: DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score. AlexNet achieved the highest AUC-ROC score (0.9816). ResNet101 showed the lowest performance across most metrics. The study notes that DenseNet201 offers the most reliable performance for practical implementation due to its balance of identifying positive and negative cases.

architecture_used.main_model: DeepLabV3+ for segmentation; AlexNet, ResNet101, and DenseNet201 for classification
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning used; models selected based on previous performance in melanoma studies and ImageNet success (AlexNet)
architecture_used.input_size: 520x520 pixels for segmentation; 224x224 pixels for CNN classification

architecture_details.segmentation_model: DeepLabV3+ with Atrous Spatial Pyramidal Pooling (ASPP) and backbones including ResNet101, DenseNet201, and AlexNet
classification_models: DenseNet201 (uses dense connections for feature reuse)

preprocessing: Oversampling of minority classes (melanoma and seborrheic keratosis) to 1450 images each

classification_or_output_head.labels: 0 for nevus, 1 for malignant melanoma, 2 for seborrheic keratosis

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing to 520x520 pixels for DeepLabV3+ segmentation
Resizing to 224x224 pixels for CNN classification input
Pixel normalization (converting pixel values from 0-255 to 0-1)
Oversampling of minority classes (melanoma and seborrheic keratosis) to 1450 images each to address class imbalance
Lesion segmentation using DeepLabV3+ to generate binary masks and isolate lesion regions from background
Data augmentation including rotation, flipping, zooming, and brightness/contrast adjustments

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to increase data variability and prevent overfitting, allowing the model to learn features under various orientations.
Applied to increase data variability and prevent overfitting.
Applied to increase data variability and prevent overfitting.
Applied to increase data variability and prevent overfitting, helping the model learn features under various lighting conditions.
Used to address class imbalance by increasing the count of minority classes (melanoma and seborrheic keratosis) to 1450 images each during the training set construction.

conclusions: DenseNet201 combined with DeepLabV3+ segmentation achieved the highest accuracy (0.9057), precision (0.9069), recall (0.9057), specificity (0.9529), and F1-score (0.9048) among the tested models, making it the most reliable for practical implementation due to its balanced performance in identifying positive and negative cases.
AlexNet achieved a competitive accuracy of 0.9011 and the highest AUC-ROC score (0.9816), serving as a strong alternative to DenseNet201 despite lower performance in other metrics.
ResNet101 demonstrated the lowest performance across most metrics (accuracy 0.8644, recall 0.8643, F1-score 0.8618) compared to DenseNet201 and AlexNet.

The integration of DeepLabV3+ for lesion segmentation prior to classification enhances feature extraction and localization, improving the precision and reliability of melanoma detection by isolating regions of interest from background noise.
The study confirms that combining DeepLabV3+ segmentation with CNN models, particularly DenseNet201, offers a viable, supportive tool for early melanoma detection.
The proposed AI systems are designed to assist medical professionals and should not replace clinical expertise.
Further evaluation on diverse datasets is necessary before deployment in real-world clinical settings.

Challenges included the limited availability of research on integrating DeepLabV3+ with classification models and the high computational cost of training (e.g., 300 epochs requiring at least 6 hours on limited GPU resources).
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, limited dataset (ISIC Melanoma Detection from Kaggle) with only 2,750 total images, raising concerns about generalizability and external validation.

Class imbalance was addressed via oversampling (melanoma and seborrheic keratosis classes increased to 1,450 images each) rather than more robust techniques like weighted loss or synthetic data generation, which may introduce bias.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of performance differences between models.
The study acknowledges high computational costs, noting that training for 300 epochs required at least 6 hours on limited GPU resources, which may hinder deployment in resource-constrained settings.
The authors explicitly state that the system is intended to assist, not replace, clinical expertise, and further evaluation on diverse datasets is necessary before real-world clinical deployment.

ResNet101 demonstrated significantly lower performance (accuracy 0.8644) compared to DenseNet201 (0.9057) and AlexNet (0.9011), suggesting that deeper architectures do not automatically yield better results in this specific pipeline without careful tuning.
AlexNet achieved the highest AUC-ROC (0.9816) despite having lower accuracy than DenseNet201, indicating potential differences in threshold-dependent performance that warrant further investigation.
